gps.help_intro: To include GPS coordinates in photo certification, location permission must be allowed in both your browser and operating system. Check the guide that matches your environment.
gps.help_ios_safari_body:
① Reset site permission (most reliable): Settings → Safari → Advanced → Website Data → search 'ori.pics' → swipe left → Delete → revisit site and tap Allow on the permission prompt
② Safari location policy: Settings → Safari → Location → Ask or Allow
③ iOS Location Services: Settings → Privacy & Security → Location Services On → Safari Websites → While Using the App + Precise Location On
gps.help_ios_chrome_body:
iOS Chrome manages permissions separately from Safari. Unlike Android Chrome, iOS Chrome does NOT have a per-site Content Settings → Location menu. Try the following in order.
① iOS system location permission (required): Settings app → Privacy & Security → Location Services On → on the same screen, find 'Chrome' → While Using the App + Precise Location On
② Reset per-site permission: Chrome bottom-right ⋯ menu → Settings → Privacy → Clear Browsing Data → Time Range: All time → check 'Cookies, Site Data' → Clear Browsing Data → revisit ori.pics, and tap Allow on the permission prompt
③ Force-quit Chrome and relaunch: open the App Switcher (swipe up from the home bar and pause) → swipe Chrome up to close it → relaunch and revisit the site
④ If still blocked, uninstall and reinstall Chrome (last resort)
gps.help_android_body:
① Chrome site permission: tap the lock icon at the left of the address bar → Permissions → Location → Allow
② OS Location: Settings → Location → On → recommend Improve accuracy mode
③ Chrome app permission: Settings → Apps → Chrome → Permissions → Location → Allow only while using the app
gps.help_desktop_body:
① Browser site permission: click the lock icon at the left of the address bar → Location → Allow
② macOS: System Settings → Privacy & Security → Location Services → enable your browser
③ Windows: Settings → Privacy → Location → enable Let apps access your location → enable Let desktop apps access your location
gps.help_https_note: ※ Geolocation is always blocked over http://. Always access the site via https://ori.pics or https://www.ori.pics.

how_it_works.title: How It Works
how_it_works.step1_desc: Upload an original image via drag & drop or file selection, or take a photo directly.
how_it_works.step2_desc: Original proof processing is performed. Nothing is stored on the server.
how_it_works.step3_desc: Download the certified image. Uploading this image again instantly verifies its authenticity. Even a single pixel change is detected.

why.title: We follow open international standards, not a walled garden
why.body:
Every proof image OriPics issues automatically carries a C2PA standard manifest.
Even if our site disappears, provenance is independently verifiable in any C2PA-aware tool.
why.point_c2pa: The content provenance and authenticity standard built by Adobe, Microsoft, Sony, BBC, and Intel — auto-attached to every proof image.
why.point_jpeg_trust: ISO/IEC 21617 (JPEG Trust) compatible. Verification responds in the Trust Report shape with an overall trust level.
why.point_open: No vendor lock-in — verifiable on contentcredentials.org, Adobe Photoshop, Verify by Truepic, and any other C2PA-aware tool.

modal.insufficient_body: Free plan allows up to 5 proofs per month. Upgrade to Pro for unlimited proofs + permanent storage.

items.a: We support most common image formats including PNG, JPG (JPEG), WebP, BMP, TIFF, and GIF. Uploaded images are automatically converted to PNG for certification processing. Maximum file size is 50MB.

items.q: How does the original certification work?
items.a: OriPics uses steganography technology to invisibly embed certification data into the pixel data of your image. When a certified image is uploaded again, the embedded data is extracted to instantly verify its authenticity. Even a single pixel modification is detected.

items.q: Can I share a certified image with others?
items.a: Yes! By creating a Quick Link, anyone can verify the original certification status in their web browser. Quick Links are valid for 7 days. Note: Sending the image file directly through social media (KakaoTalk, Instagram, WhatsApp, etc.) may break the certification, as these platforms automatically compress and resize images. To preserve the certification, we recommend sharing via Quick Link.

items.q: Are my images stored on the server?
items.a: During certification, your image is never sent to the server. Only if you create a Quick Link, the certified result image is stored securely in cloud storage for 7 days before being automatically deleted.

No. OriPics converts to lossless PNG format for certification, so there is absolutely no quality degradation. Only imperceptible data is embedded — indistinguishable from the original to the human eye.

items.q: What happens when I upload an animated image (GIF, WebP, APNG, etc.)?
items.a: Animated images such as GIF, animated WebP, and APNG contain multiple frames, but OriPics certifies only the first frame. The certified result is saved as a static PNG image. Animation is not preserved.

items.q: Is GPS location included when I take a photo?
items.a: Yes. When you tap 'Take Photo' on mobile and allow location access, your GPS coordinates at the moment of capture are included as part of the certification. Location data is embedded only in the image pixels and is not stored separately on the server. File upload and clipboard methods do not include GPS to prevent tampering.

items.q: GPS coordinates aren't saved after taking a photo on iPhone Safari — which settings should I check?
items.a: Check these four in order. ① [Reset site permission — most reliable] Settings → Safari → Advanced → Website Data → search 'ori.pics' → swipe the entry left and Delete → revisit the site and tap Allow when the permission prompt reappears. ② [Safari-wide location policy] Settings → Safari → Location → set to Ask or Allow (Deny blocks every site). ③ [iOS system location services] Settings → Privacy & Security → Location Services → On → scroll down to 'Safari Websites' → While Using the App + Precise Location turned on. ④ [HTTPS required] Geolocation is always blocked over http://. Always access the site via https://ori.pics or https://www.ori.pics.

items.q: Does editing EXIF metadata (timestamp, GPS) with an EXIF editor affect OriPics certification?
items.a: No, it has zero effect. The timestamp, GPS, and resolution that OriPics certifies are embedded directly into the image pixels via steganography — not stored in EXIF. EXIF is just plain text metadata that anyone can freely edit or delete, but OriPics verification ignores EXIF entirely and extracts only the original information encoded in the pixels. So no matter how you alter the EXIF values, the verified timestamp and GPS will always show the original values recorded at the moment of certification. And if even a single pixel is modified, it is instantly detected as tampering.

items.q: Are high-resolution images certified at original size?
items.a: Images with the longest side exceeding 1800 pixels are automatically resized (proportionally) to fit within 1800 pixels before certification (e.g., 4032×3024 → 1800×1350). This is to speed up mobile download and sharing — the resized image becomes the certified original. Images at 1800 pixels or smaller are certified at their original size. If you need certification at full original resolution, please contact us.
